title: Review of A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain
author: Kevan Myers
orientation: Kevan Myers reviews the documentary A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain, calling it a Spirit-led clarion call that documents the rise of the modern Abolition Movement and confronts pro-life Christians with the contrast between pro-life rhetoric and biblical abolitionism. He urges Bible-believing Christians to watch, weigh the film's testimonies and tenets against Scripture, and act.
audiences: Pastor weighing the church's response to abortion

questions.question: What is the central thesis Myers wants pro-life Christians to confront when watching this documentary?
questions.answer: Myers argues the film exposes an inconsistency between what pro-lifers say they believe about the personhood of the preborn and what pro-life legislation actually does. The documentary lays out how typical pro-life bills fail to treat the preborn as equally valuable to the born, even though pro-lifers verbally affirm that equality.
questions.quote: the film expertly shows that pro-life legislation is not consistent with what most pro-lifers say they believe.

questions.question: How does the film define Abolitionism, and what mnemonic structures its tenets?
questions.answer: The documentary frames Abolitionism as the doctrine answering how the Bride of Christ should combat great societal evil. Pastor Dusty Deevers organizes the five tenets into the acrostic G.A.T.E.S., and the film walks through each tenet one at a time.
questions.quote: In the film, Pastor Dusty Deevers puts the five tenets of Abolitionism into an acrostic, saying “Abolitionists have their G.A.T.E.S.”

questions.question: What specific critique does Bradley Pierce voice in the film against the Pro-Life Movement's legislative record?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing the church's response to abortion
questions.answer: Bradley Pierce says that while the Pro-Life Movement has spent nearly five decades affirming preborn personhood verbally, the bills the pro-life lobby promotes do not legally treat preborn human beings as equally valuable to those already born. The rhetoric and the legislation, in his telling, do not match.
questions.quote: the bills that the pro-life lobby has been introducing and pushing and calling on all of the grassroots to support don’t actually do that. They don’t actually treat a person before they are born as equally as valuable as a person after they are born.

questions.question: What range of activism does the film visually depict?
questions.answer: Myers notes the documentary captures an unusually wide spectrum of testimony and on-the-ground engagement. It moves from sidewalk witness at abortion mills to settings inside churches and statehouses, and from intimate family conversations to large-scale public demonstrations.
questions.quote: it shows a spectrum of engagement from footage outside abortion mills to houses of worship and legislatures, from living rooms to street engagement and massive rallies.

questions.question: How does Myers describe his emotional response to viewing the film?
questions.answer: Even as a longtime abolitionist who anticipated the film favorably, Myers reports being deeply moved on watching it. He describes recurring tears as he heard pastors and laypeople speak hard truths in love and humility, including testimonies of repentance over abortion, apathy, or resistance to abolition.
questions.quote: I was smitten afresh and blown away by the message and delivery of *A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain*. Tears kept springing to my eyes again and again as I heard piercing truths, spoken in love and humility, by brothers and sisters in Christ.

questions.question: What kinds of repentance does the documentary highlight among Christians it features?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing the church's response to abortion
questions.answer: The film foregrounds testimonies of Christians, including pastors, who have been brought to repentance over distinct failings: a personal abortion in the past, apathetic inaction in the present, or defending pro-life methodology while resisting biblical abolitionist responses to abortion.
questions.quote: fellow Christians, including pastors, share how God brought them to repentance, whether it be over an abortion in their past, or apathetic inaction, or clinging to pro-lifeism and fighting against the Biblical response to the abortion Holocaust.

questions.question: What does Myers ask viewers to do after watching the film?
questions.answer: Myers issues a four-step charge: watch the film, weigh its message against Scripture, listen to testimonies and consider the tenets, and if the case holds up biblically, embrace it, act on it, and share the film widely.
questions.quote: Listen to the testimonies. Weigh out the tenets. If this is according to the Scriptures, then embrace it and take action, and pass on this film far and wide!

questions.question: Why does Myers describe the present moment as a turning point for serious Christians?
questions.answer: Myers opens the review by framing abolitionism as a swelling, audible movement that demands the attention of Bible-believing Christians. He treats the documentary as evidence that the movement has reached a stage of visibility and momentum that should not be ignored.
questions.quote: It is happening! The thunder of Abolitionism is rumbling across the land. And serious, Bible believing Christians need to take notice.

related.title: Timothy Cole: My Conversion From Pro-Life Speaker to Abolitionist
related.reason: A first-person testimony of the same kind of pro-life-to-abolitionist conversion the documentary documents and Myers celebrates.

related.title: My Pro-Life-to-Abolitionist Conversion
related.reason: Another conversion narrative that reinforces the film's central pattern of repentance from pro-lifeism toward abolitionism.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Expands on Bradley Pierce's critique in the film that pro-life legislation fails to treat the preborn as equally valuable.

related.title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
related.reason: Provides background on the conflict between the Pro-Life Movement and abolitionism that the documentary depicts.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Directly addresses the identity distinction the film urges Christians to make between pro-lifeism and abolitionism.

related.title: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
related.reason: A parallel review of an abolitionist documentary, useful for readers interested in how the movement uses film as a tool for cultural confrontation.
